data.title: Energy And The AI Buildout: An Investor's Perspective
data.description:
By Tony Kim & Tomas Hamudis

Scarce Today, Abundant Tomorrow: Power and the AI Buildout

We are in the middle of what could be a once-in-a-generation industrial buildout. It requires semiconductors, equipment, labor, data centers, and massive amounts of power. Power is one of the bottlenecks, but the system is adapting across the short and long term. We are building the physical foundation for scaling machine intelligence and accelerating the next wave of productivity growth.

What Is the Demand From Data Centers?

We expect approximately 148GW of additional power capacity will be needed by the end of the decade to satisfy data center demand, more than quadrupling the roughly 42GW in power capacity consumed by data centers in 2025. Including non-data center load growth, we expect power demand to rise by 23GW in 2026 and 43GW in 2027.

Data centers to require 148GW of additional capacity this decade

Forecasted U.S. Power Demand

How Did We Get Here?

Since the launch of ChatGPT in late 2022, AI compute investments have risen massively, tracking to exceed $1T per year. Even so, demand for compute has been systematically underestimated. As AI evolves from simple queries to multimodal systems and persistent agents, across more users, and with longer runtimes, compute demand compounds. Efficiency gains have lowered unit costs, but aggregate demand has scaled even faster, making electricity a key factor in AI deployment.

What is the Urgency?

We believe the economics are decisive. Our research shows that a 1GW AI data center can cost roughly $40B to build, including equipment such as GPUs and networking systems, yet can possibly generate more than $12B of annual high margin revenue almost immediately. Power is the gate to that cash flow. But the urgency is not only economic. AI is also a strategic priority. In this environment, power develops a yield curve: capacity available today could be worth far more than capacity delivered years from now.

What is the Power Bottleneck?

Current indications suggest that the system is nearly sold out. There is very little incremental AI power capacity available over the next two years beyond what is already under construction or in advanced development. Grid interconnection queues are congested. Gas turbine deliveries extend into 2030. Transformers and switchgear are in short supply. Engineering, procurement, and construction crews are in high demand.

Will Power Constraints Stop the AI Data Center Buildout?

Based on our comprehensive review of ongoing and future power supply needs for the AI data center buildout, the answer is no. Near-term constraints are being addressed through pragmatic solutions. In the out years of 2029-2030 we see incentives and innovation as likely combining to meet modest shortfalls. The system is adjusting across multiple time horizons, solving for immediate gaps while expanding the structural base.

A Collision Between Two Worlds: Tech and Power

The increasing use of power to build AI Infrastructure has forced a collision between two radically different operating cultures.

  • The AI ecosystem, including labs, hyperscalers, and semiconductor companies, moves quickly, with competitive and financial incentives for rapid iteration, aggressive capital deployment and first-mover advantage.
  • The power complex, including utilities, grid operators, regional markets, and equipment manufacturers, has seen little structural growth for nearly two decades and is optimized for reliability, regulatory compliance, capital preservation, and risk minimization.

When unprecedented demand collides with slow-moving, risk-averse institutions, strain is inevitable. The response is creativity. The AI ecosystem is shifting to new geographies, underwriting long-term contracts, and pushing supply chains to expand. Developers are also reducing grid dependence by building generation alongside compute, a trend known as “Behind the Meter” or “Bring Your Own Power (BYOP)”. Projects launch with anchor capacity and scale in phases, accelerating capacity beyond what the grid can deliver.

Case Studies: New Models for Powering AI

xAI in Memphis, Tennessee

xAI built one of the largest AI compute clusters in the world, deploying 100,000 GPUs in roughly 122 days. The site operates at close to 1GW of power capacity, comparable to the electricity demand of a major city. Faced with multi-year grid interconnection timelines, xAI moved quickly, deploying road portable natural gas turbines under temporary permits. The site integrates batteries, mobile generation, and grid supply in a layered system designed for load variability. Launch fast, then optimize.

Stargate in Abilene, Texas

The data center campus in Abilene, Texas, part of the Stargate project and developed with Crusoe, reflects the next phase of large-scale AI infrastructure. The campus was developed with a 1.2GW grid interconnection, behind-the-meter energy resources, including natural gas turbines to support on-site generation. The campus is now live, with expansion underway toward its planned 1.2GW of total capacity. West Texas offers abundant land, existing energy infrastructure, and a supportive regulatory environment. This is not only bridge power, it is vertically integrated infrastructure purpose built for AI.

Case studies are shown for illustrative and educational purposes only. They do not represent BlackRock investments or strategies and are not a prediction of future results. Outcomes may differ materially depending on market, regulatory, and operational factors. References to specific companies are for illustrative purposes only and should not be construed as investment advice or investment recommendations.

Can the System Support the Current View on Demand?

In the near term, yes. Based on our exhaustive analysis, we conclude that the power supply systems fueling AI are more flexible than commonly assumed. Gas turbine manufacturing is already scaling and can accelerate further, solar is growing rapidly, and emerging technologies such as fuel cells are contributing. Batteries play a growing and meaningful role by raising the yield of power-generating sources. Through 2026–2027, available capacity, ongoing buildouts, and interim solutions appear sufficient. After 2027, the system is more stretched, but uncertainty on both sides is higher.

Power pressures grow, but solutions emerging

US Power Surplus

Power supply systems fueling AI are more flexible than assumed

Forecasted U.S. Power Supply

  • Natural gas is the primary source of incremental AI-grade power over the next several years, particularly in regions with existing pipeline infrastructure. In 2026–2027, supply may come from higher utilization of the existing fleet, slower retirements, and increasingly net new gas additions. New-build gas capacity could ramp more materially in 2027–2028 and beyond as turbine deliveries accelerate. Turbine manufacturers are expanding capacity, supported by U.S. policy aimed at increasing output, and we see upside to current production plans. We expect approximately +20GW of net new gas capacity in 2026–2027 and +61GW through the end of the decade.
  • Solar is large, growing rapidly, and highly cost-competitive. It can be deployed faster than gas in many instances and, given intermittency, is deployed as part of the grid or with battery storage systems. Global manufacturing capacity is ample, albeit concentrated in Asia. We expect approximately +14GW of net new solar capacity in 2026–2027 and +35GW through 2030, with additional upside if demand accelerates.
  • Wind adds utility-scale, cost-competitive generation in high-resource regions with existing transmission. While development timelines can be longer than solar, wind complements solar output and improves overall grid reliability. We expect approximately +6GW in 2026–2027 and +14GW through 2030.
  • Batteries (stationary storage) expand power supply by improving utilization of existing assets. Storage enables balancing and peak support, allowing operators to defer or avoid other investments. Storage is quick to deploy and is a critical tool supporting demand growth. We estimate batteries can support approximately +9GW in 2026–2027 and +27GW through 2030.
  • Bring Your Own Power (BYOP) has emerged as a source of incremental supply that did not exist at scale 18 months ago. Some projects are true standalone power islands using large gas turbines, while turbine scarcity has driven modular solutions including smaller gas turbines, reciprocating engines, batteries, and fuel cells. Together, these approaches have created a new layer of supply that can be brought online faster than conventional grid-connected generation. We estimate BYOP deployments can support approximately +5GW in 2026–2027 and +20GW through the end of the decade, with upside if grid constraints persist and demand exceeds expectations.
  • Coal retirement schedules are not immutable. When grid reliability is at stake, timelines can shift, as recent emergency orders delaying plant closures amid rising data center load demonstrate. Existing coal plants represent supply that can remain online for years if economics and policy align. We expect retirements of 5GW net capacity in 2026–2027 and 20GW through the end of the decade.

BlackRock Fundamental Equities, with data from Bloomberg New Energy Finance and US Energy Information Administration as of February 24, 2026. Chart shows power generation net capacity broken down by fuel source (Nuclear, Wind, Bring-Your-Own-Power, Battery, Solar, Gas, and Coal + Other).

How Is the Ecosystem Adapting?

Generation is only part of the adjustment. The AI buildout is reshaping geography, capital flows, infrastructure design, and policy.

  • Location, location, location? Historically, data centers clustered near population centers to minimize latency. Today, much of the incremental build, particularly large-scale AI training clusters, is moving toward power-rich regions such as West Texas, where large blocks of capacity can be secured and developed faster.
  • Capital is underwriting supply. Hyperscalers are no longer passive buyers of electricity. Through long-dated contracts and development partnerships, they are anchoring new generation projects and accelerating capacity.
  • Stranded infrastructure is being repriced. Decommissioned power plants, idle industrial sites, and crypto mining facilities with existing transmission or fuel access are now among the fastest paths to incremental AI-grade power.
  • Policy is compressing timelines and expanding supply. AI is now a strategic priority. Regulators are rethinking interconnection processes and ensuring hyperscalers fund infrastructure. Governments are also pushing manufacturers of turbines and other critical equipment to expand production capacity.

What Long-Term Solutions Could Expand Supply in the 2030s?

As we move into the 2030s, two developments have the potential to materially change how AI infrastructure is powered and scaled.

  • Nuclear energy is one of the few clean, always-on sources capable of supporting large, concentrated AI loads. Hyperscalers are already contracting for output tied to extending, refurbishing, or restarting existing plants. Small modular reactors (SMRs) offer a model that could be deployed closer to data centers. Sustained AI demand is increasing political and financial support for new-build nuclear, positioning nuclear as a critical pillar of AI power beyond 2030.
  • Data centers in space are moving from theory to exploration. SpaceX has proposed orbital data centers powered by continuous solar exposure, leveraging natural cooling and eliminating terrestrial grid constraints. Google, Amazon, and others are evaluating similar concepts. With reusable launch systems reducing launch costs and increasing payload capacity, deploying large-scale orbital compute over time is technically plausible, introducing an entirely new supply frontier.

The Bigger Prize

Every megawatt unlocked expands our capacity to scale machine intelligence. This is not simply an infrastructure story. It is a productivity story. For most of modern history, labor has accounted for more than half of global GDP. AI has the potential to alter the relationship between labor, capital, and output itself. That is why the power constraint should be solved. The prize is too consequential and too strategically visible for it not to be.

Conclusion

Power constraints are unlikely to derail AI data center deployment on their own. Over the next two years, the gap is bridged by a mix of in-flight capacity, gas-led additions, storage, BYOP, and site re-optimization. Beyond 2027, the challenge becomes less about “is there enough power?” and more about how quickly the system can translate capital into energized capacity.

Importantly, these frictions create a self-regulating mechanism: they cap the pace of expansion, prioritize the highest-return projects, and reduce the risk of indiscriminate overbuilding.

For investors, that means the opportunity is shifting from a broad AI rally to more selective stock exposure. In an environment with dynamic bottlenecks and timelines, dispersion should rise, and we believe an active, nimble approach is best positioned to capture the winners.

Sources

[Power Supply Chart] Source: BlackRock Fundamental Equities, with data from Bloomberg New Energy Finance and US Energy Information Administration as of February 24, 2026. Chart shows power generation net capacity broken down by fuel source (Nuclear, Wind, Bring-Your-Own-Power, Battery, Solar, Gas, and Coal + Other).

[Power Demand Chart] Source: BlackRock Fundamental Equities, with data from US Energy Information Administration as of October 16, 2025 and from Semianalysis as of January 12, 2026. Chart shows US power demand by sector (Data center and Other).

[Power Surplus Chart] Source: BlackRock Fundamental Equities, with data from Bloomberg New Energy Finance and US Energy Information Administration as of February 24, 2026, and Semianalysis as of January 12, 2026. Chart shows surplus/deficit of US net power generation capacity in excess of US power demand.

This post originally appeared on BlackRock

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Apple: I See An Alchain-Allen Theorem At Play (Upgrade)
data.description:
Thesis review

The hardware and electronics market is facing a margin headwind that's being caused by the supply shortage of DRAM and NAND. This shortage is estimated to persist for the duration of 2026 and maybe last until 2027. This headwind has painted a bad margin picture for firms that have memory as one of their input costs; Dell (DELL), Xiaomi (XIACY), and Apple (AAPL) are a few of the names expected to experience lower gross margins in the subsequent quarters. As a result, I figured it would be prudent to review my initial thesis on Apple. In my previous article, I was refuting the ongoing bear thesis that said iPhone sales declines will result in Apple's valuation contracting. My rebuttal was that given the revenue growth being witnessed in the services segment, Apple's bottom line will grow even higher, sustaining shareholder yield. Since the publication of my article, the stock has moved in line with the broader market.

Even looking at the YoY return, investors would have been better off holding the (SPY) than the Apple stock when adjusting for the concentration risk. This performance is simply disappointing for me and illustrative of the fact that Apple currently trades at fair value and the market doesn't see further upside. Meanwhile, underneath, I see a growth catalyst that makes me even more bullish on the name.

Bull Case

Contrary to the popular view, I actually see the memory shortage as a growth catalyst for Apple. The reason being, the shortage is going to push other hardware providers (with less pricing power) to increase prices to their consumers, reducing the price differential between standard and premium hardware (Apple products); this reduction will create a higher demand for Apple's products as the quality of the products will now be more attractive. This phenomenon is called the Alchian-Allen theorem, and we are already seeing signs of it at play. In Q1 of 2026, Apple led the global shipment of smartphones (5% YoY growth) while the overall market declined, with Xiaomi recording one of the highest drops of 19% YoY. During the Q1 earnings call, the analyst from TD Cowen asked if it was possible that the record numbers that were hit in Q1 were due to the memory constraints, and from Tim Cook's response, it's likely that is what we are witnessing.

The market hasn't fully priced this growth catalyst in, and this is reflected by forward revenue estimates marking lower than YoY growth. I agree that we are likely to see a minor margin hit in the coming quarters, but topline growth will likely top the current estimates as the install base continues to hit record numbers across all geographies. I now feel confident to upgrade Apple to a Strong Buy.

Momentum

As I pointed out earlier, Apple's performance hasn't been that impressive; over the period of 3 years, the stock only appreciated by 63.5%, while its peers grew by triple digits and the SPY grew by 72%. YTD the stock is still trailing at -0.6%, while the SPY is up by more than 4%. I think the stock simply needs a growth catalyst that will validate a premium valuation, and I think we are now at that inflection point.

Financial Analysis

Q1 2026 Income Statement

Looking at the last quarter's earnings results, Apple's revenue grew 16% to $143.76 billion, beating estimates by $5.23 billion. The last time Apple had an estimate beat of such magnitude was in Q1 of 2021. The growth was led by iPhone sales growing 23% to $85.27 billion, followed by services growing 14% to $30.01 billion. Mac sales offset growth with a 7% decline to $8.60 billion. Cost of revenue grew 13% to $74.53 billion; the growth was led by the cost of product increasing 14% to $67.48 billion, while the cost of services increased by only 7% to $7.05 billion. This resulted in gross profits increasing 19% to $69.23 billion and marking gross margins of 48.16%. Product gross margins increased 450 bps QoQ to 40.7%, while services gross margins increased 120 bps QoQ to 76.5%, both driven by favorable mix and leverage. Operating expenses increased 19% to $18.38 billion, driven by a significant growth in R&D spending to the value of $10.89 billion. This led to operating income growing 19% to $50.85 billion and pretax income growing 20% to $51.01 billion. Income tax increased by 42%, resulting in net income increasing 16% to $42.10 billion and GAAP EPS increasing to $2.84, beating estimates by $0.17.

Apple's margin profile signifies the firm's pricing power in relation to its peers. The scatterplot below shows how Apple's margins are far above its industry peers in the electronics and hardware space. Apple's gross margin slightly trails the sector margin of 49.98%; however, it significantly exceeds the sector net margins of 5.27%.

Q1 Cash Flow Statement

Operating cash flow grew 80% to $53.93 billion, with the growth bolstered by a cash inflow of $5.55 billion from net working capital. Free cash flow grew 90% to $47.96 billion, driven by CAPEX shrinking 19% to $2.37 billion and offset by SBC increasing 9% to $3.59 billion.

With EBIT margins of 32.28%, Apple exceeds its sector margin of 6.81% and peers like Dell, with an EBIT margin of 7.68%, and Xiaomi, with an EBIT margin of 7.49%. FCF margins are also impressive at 24.41% vs. the sector margin of 11.14%. These incredible profitability metrics have resulted in the Quant system granting it a profitability grade of A+. Given the low-margin profile Apple peers have, the memory shortage will likely corner these firms to pass through the rising input costs to their end consumers, inevitably increasing the demand for Apple's products.

Q1 2026 Balance Sheet

Apple's balance sheet remains in great condition, with cash reserves increasing 50% to $45.3 billion while inventories decreased 15% to $5.9 billion. This significant decrease in inventories is also illustrative of the increasing demand for Apple's products but also shows the ongoing shortage in input components and supply chain disruptions. Current debt increased 9% to $11.8 billion, while long-term debt decreased 9% to $76.7 billion, landing total debt at $91 billion and debt to FCF at 1.67. Apple is a cash-generative powerhouse, and the short-term margin compression shows little threat to the firm's shareholder yield.

Valuation

The Seeking Quant system gave Apple a valuation grade of F, implying a premium valuation, but this is mostly driven by the price-to-book multiple, which is slightly misleading as book value doesn't accurately reflect Apple's earnings growth. Looking at other multiples, we can see that Apple trades in line with its peers and sector. Apple has an EBITDA multiple of 25.39 and a cash flow multiple of 29.28, slightly above the sector EBITDA multiple of 19.15 and a cash flow multiple of 18.76. The chart below also shows Apple slightly positioned lower than a trendline, implying the cash flow multiple could be lower than it's deserved.

Analyzing the valuation beyond its industry peers, I made a screen of all tech stocks that trade for a PE ranging between 30 and 50, and then I made a scatter plot that compares the FCF multiple vs. operating margin. The results below show that Apple still maintains higher operating margins than most names in this basket; however, the FCF multiple is in line with the trend at 32.50x (also in line with its 3-year rolling median). This solidifies the point that Apple looks fairly valued, and given the ongoing weak momentum, the market doesn't see further upside.

Q2 Outlook

I strongly believe that topline growth, especially coming from the product segment, will push multiples to the premium level, a well-deserved valuation for a brand name like Apple with a monopolistic position and a high margin profile. While the memory shortage didn't compress gross margins in Q1, management stated that the impact will be slightly higher in Q2 but still guided for a margin range of 48%-49%, implying that topline growth and the services segment will largely offset the margin compression from the product side. The revenue guide ranged from 13% to 16%, with constraints in iPhone supply priced in. In a week from now, Apple is going to release Q2 earnings results, I suggest investors pay attention to the product segment growth, as this is the make-or-break point of my strong buy thesis. The growth in product sales would be validating my high-demand expectation from end consumers.

Risk

Looking at the risk profile, I want to start by saying that upgrading Apple to a strong buy is a highly speculative take, as this name is not insulated from the memory shortage headwind. Just because there could be a growing demand for Apple's products, this doesn't imply the demand will immediately translate to sales, as we all know that for that to happen, the demand must be met with adequate supply. This will simply not happen if the impact of memory shortage is significantly higher than my expectations. Another risk is that in recent news, Tim Cook is expected to step down as Apple's CEO in H2 of 2026. This adds a management risk that could impact Apple stock; in fact, after the news broke, the stock fell by 0.8%.

Conclusion

In conclusion, I'm upgrading Apple to a strong buy, as I see the shortage in memory could catalyze the growth of the product segment. This is the business segment that has received a healthy amount of criticism and laid a bear case for the stock in recent years. While I don't see higher margin contributions in the short term, the growth will eventually be margin accretive as the product segment serves as an onboarding mechanism to more service growth, the segment that has higher margins. Even though we might not see margin growth in 2026, I expect to see it once the memory shortage issue has been resolved, which is estimated to be around 2027.

data.title: Equity LifeStyle Properties: Fairly Valued Given Seasonal Pressures
data.description:
Shares of Equity Lifestyle Properties (ELS) have been a disappointing performer over the past year, trading essentially flat and missing out on a meaningful equity market rally. The stock has been held back by a full valuation and pressure in its seasonal business, especially with less travel from Canadians into the United States. That said, the company reported solid Q1 results, and some of its cost pressures may be fading. I last covered shares in December, where I rated the stock a suitable “Hold” in portfolios but held back from a "Buy" rating. With a 7% return since then, that call was overly cautious, and a "Buy" was merited. With updated financials, now is a good time to revisit shares.

In the company’s first quarter, Equity Lifestyle generated $0.84 of funds from operations, which beat estimates by a penny as revenue grew 3% to $398 million. Core FFO was up just 0.3% from last year as higher interest expenses largely offset a 1.8% increase in EBITDA to $201 million. Core property revenue grew 3.7%, while expenses increased 4.9%, but I expect cost inflation to improve over the course of the year.

While mobile homes are less than 76,000 of its 173,000 sites, they do drive the majority of the business, at about 63% of revenue. 91% of ELS’s revenue is annual, leading to steady cash flow, though seasonal activity drives near-term variances. The company operates across 35 states, but Florida, California, and Arizona are the majority of its business, with Florida over a third all by itself. These Sun Belt markets have enjoyed meaningful domestic migration as well as an influx of retirees. These trends have supported demand for mobile homes, especially with lower initial ownership costs than a single-family home, making them attractive for retirees in particular.

In its mobile home unit, base rental income was up a solid 5.7% from last year. Units were up about 0.5% from last year to 73,600, and the company continues to pass on rent increases to tenants. Rents were up about 6% to $948/month. However, occupancy was down 60 bps from last year. ELS has consistently increased rent, and while mobile homes are still much more affordable than single-family homes, this may be starting to limit demand, especially as migration to Florida has slowed.

Aside from this, its RV and Marina unit continues to struggle, and an unseasonably cold winter across the Southeast was a further headwind. Core income declined by 1.4% from last year to $114.5 million. Annual sales were up 4% to $80 million. However, seasonal activity fell by 15% to $23 million, with transient activity down 7%. While annual activity provides a significant base of revenue, seasonal activity is more economically sensitive and continues to be weak. Seasonal activity has generally been soft from less Canadian tourism, and I expect that to remain the case given the souring of geopolitical relations.

Additionally, higher fuel costs, given the war in Iran, may further reduce discretionary travel and vacations. While not an impact on the annual business, it can make for further weakness in seasonal activity, and as such, I see risks of further weakness. Fuel economy can be 8-22 mpg, a relatively low level, so higher prices, combined with ongoing affordability challenges, may reduce revenue. I continue to believe that markets are underpricing the risk from the Strait of Hormuz closure and lost oil supply.

Importantly, ELS can make up some of this pressure on the expense side. On April 1, it renewed its insurance program and saw an 18% decrease in premiums. Thanks to legislative changes in Florida and a quiet hurricane season in 2025, we have seen meaningful disinflation in insurance costs after significant increases post-2020. This should help to drive expenses lower over the balance of the year. Insurance costs were flat in Q1 at $27 million, and they should run below $25 million over the rest of 2026. This saving can help to offset the weakness in the RV and marina unit.

One risk to be mindful of is utilities account for about 28% of expenses, and only half of this is recovered, leaving ~14% of expenses as an ELS exposure. Utility prices have risen over the past few years, and there are concerns that incremental data center demand could raise electricity prices further. This remains to be seen, and more recently, natural gas prices have come down, which should alleviate some of the upward pressure on utility costs. Given this, I do not see utility costs being an upside risk in 2026, though some longer-term inflation is possible.

Equity Lifestyle maintains a solid balance sheet with $3.3 billion of debt. Debt/EBITDA ended the quarter at 4.5x, flat sequentially and up just slightly from 4.4x a year ago. I like to see leverage below 5x, and ELS is comfortably below this level, providing plenty of headroom for bolt-on M&A should opportunities arise. Plus, it has no maturities in 2026 and just $200 million next year, limiting its near-term sensitivity to interest rates. Given the health of its balance sheet, I view its dividend as secure, and shares offer a 3.3% dividend yield.

Alongside results, ELS reaffirmed full-year core FFO guidance of $3.12-$3.22. However, it reduced core growth rates a bit, reflecting ongoing pressure in seasonal activity. Its mobile home forecast is unchanged at 5.6%, but RV and marina are expected to grow just 2.4% from 2.9%. Fortunately, it can offset this with lower property expenses. Back in December, I was targeting at least $3.20 in 2026 FFO, so the company’s outlook is not that different from my own. I now see results in the $3.16-$3.22 area, given some continued sluggishness in seasonal demand.

As has been the case for some time, Equity Lifestyle scores poorly on Seeking Alpha’s valuation screener. Now, the real estate sector faces many challenges, and while not perfect, I view ELS’s fundamentals as being stronger than many subsectors. For instance, office and mall real estate face significant secular pressure and lower multiples as a result. While the multifamily sector has strong long-term fundamentals in my view, apartment rental inflation is currently fragile given a surge of construction activity post-COVID. In contrast, mobile home rental inflation remains solidly positive and does not face secular decline. ELS also maintains a conservative balance sheet.

In my view, these factors merit a premium valuation to the sector, and I would not "Sell" ELS simply because it trades at a premium to the real estate sector. I expect this premium to be fairly structural in nature. Shares offer a ~4.9% FFO yield, and I see about 3%-4% long-term growth given modest unit count growth and above-inflation rent increases. As such, ELS can provide up to an ~8% long-term return, especially as its balance sheet provides flexibility for M&A, but I struggle to see much more than that.

Over time, that positions ELS to be a market performer rather than an outperformer, in my view. As such, I continue to see shares as a suitable "Hold" in portfolios, but I see no rush to "Buy." Given its largely annual revenue stream and normalizing insurance costs, I am not a seller, and ELS provides a sustainable combination of income and income growth. That said, I would want to see shares retrace towards $60 before buying more and would look elsewhere for better upside opportunities.
